coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of Eu3+/Tb3+-co-doped zirconia electrospun nanofibers as a multicolored photoluminescence sensor for the determination of levodopa

Emadi, Hamid
Nabavi, Seyed Reza
coredata.dc:description: In this study, ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles were synthesized by a microwave-assisted approach to determine levodopa, the precursor of the neurotransmitter dopamine. Dopamine, as a catecholamine, can pass across the blood-brain barrier to enter the central nervous system and treat Parkinson's disease. The identification of levodopa's harmful effects has enabled the development of a variety of analytical approaches. The fluorescence sensor was employed in this study due to its advantageous characteristics, including precise measurement capabilities and rapid response times. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, and UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers were employed to examine the optical, microscopic, and spectroscopic characteristics of these nanoparticles. The sensor's efficiency was evaluated by detecting levodopa with a recovery percentage of 86–99 % in human blood serum and 97–102 % in urine from actual human samples. The mechanism in the interaction between levodopa and nanoparticles was explored using UV–vis spectroscopy, which indicated that the PET mechanism was responsible for the observed quenching. The sensitivity of the sensor was also determined by the lowest detection limit of 91 nM, which was achieved by examining the effects of levodopa on the sensor in the linear range of 1–100 µM. Furthermore, the sensor exhibited excellent stability and selectivity. The sensor was constructed using electrospun polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers, which were deemed more innovative and practical than paper substrates. Additionally, an orange-emitting ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ sensor based on PAN was employed to assess levodopa visually.

head.ce:title: Fabrication of Eu3+/Tb3+-co-doped zirconia electrospun nanofibers as a multicolored photoluminescence sensor for the determination of levodopa

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles were synthesized by a microwave-assisted approach to determine levodopa, the precursor of the neurotransmitter dopamine. Dopamine, as a catecholamine, can pass across the blood-brain barrier to enter the central nervous system and treat Parkinson's disease. The identification of levodopa's harmful effects has enabled the development of a variety of analytical approaches. The fluorescence sensor was employed in this study due to its advantageous characteristics, including precise measurement capabilities and rapid response times. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy, energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy, diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, and UV–vis and fluorescence spectrophotometers were employed to examine the optical, microscopic, and spectroscopic characteristics of these nanoparticles. The sensor's efficiency was evaluated by detecting levodopa with a recovery percentage of 86–99 % in human blood serum and 97–102 % in urine from actual human samples. The mechanism in the interaction between levodopa and nanoparticles was explored using UV–vis spectroscopy, which indicated that the PET mechanism was responsible for the observed quenching. The sensitivity of the sensor was also determined by the lowest detection limit of 91 nM, which was achieved by examining the effects of levodopa on the sensor in the linear range of 1–100 µM. Furthermore, the sensor exhibited excellent stability and selectivity. The sensor was constructed using electrospun polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers, which were deemed more innovative and practical than paper substrates. Additionally, an orange-emitting ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ sensor based on PAN was employed to assess levodopa visually.

Quenched fluorescence of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles in interaction with different levodopa concentrations acting as an electron acceptor due to dynamic mechanism
Image, graphical abstract

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Levodopa (L-DOPA), a dopamine precursor, is a key therapy to slow Parkinson's disease progression, often combined with agents like ropinirole, rasagiline, rotigotine, and selegiline. These medications, including adjunctive drugs such as entacapone and apomorphine, enhance treatment efficacy by targeting dopamine pathways. Their demonstrated effectiveness positions them as vital therapeutic options for managing Parkinson's symptoms [1]. A significant class of neurotransmitters, catecholamines, includes noradrenaline, adrenaline, levodopa, and dopamine. An intermediate in the dopamine production pathway is the naturally generated levodopa amino acid [2]. Elevations of catecholamines have been identified as cardiotoxic, meaning they can cause hypertension, a fast heartbeat, and even cardiac muscle death. Conversely, certain severe illnesses, such as Parkinson's disease, may arise from the loss of DA-containing neurons.

Levodopa detection in biological fluids employs various analytical methods, including capillary electrophoresis (CE), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), ion chromatography (IC), ultraviolet spectrophotometry (UV), and electrochemical methods [3]. For example, Wu and colleagues employed capillary electrophoresis with laser-induced native fluorescence to monitor DA, adrenaline, and noradrenaline in urine. This approach enables the investigation of these substances at the sub-nanomolar level [4]. Although several of these techniques are widely used and demonstrate good sensitivity and selectivity for levodopa detection, their limitations often outweigh their advantages. Many of these methods suffer from significant drawbacks, such as low sensitivity, poor selectivity, limited stability, high costs, the need for highly skilled operators, and extensive sample pretreatment, making them unsuitable for real-time and on-site detection. In contrast, fluorescence sensors have emerged as a highly practical optical detection technique, effectively addressing these challenges and offering a robust alternative for rapid and reliable analysis [5]. Among the numerous advantages of optical sensing, the high sensitivity and selectivity are of particular significance. Furthermore, the device exhibits resistance to electromagnetic interference and resilience in the face of high temperatures and pressures. Fluorescence measurement is conducted using specific excitation and emission wavelengths to target analytes [6]. Accurate detection and quantification of biological/chemical compounds are crucial for diverse applications like clinical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and security. Sensors enable rapid, efficient identification and differentiation of multiple target analytes [7]. Nanomaterials exhibit considerable potential due to their unique optical, catalytic, magnetic, and electrical properties. Moreover, superior control over the analytical performance of sensors is one of the properties of a wide range of nanomaterials, due to the ease of development and flexible characteristics [8].

Lanthanide-doped inorganic nanoparticles feature a complex host lattice structure, where lanthanides integrate into specific sites, optimizing their optical performance. This precise positioning within the crystalline framework significantly enhances luminescence efficiency. Metal oxides are highly adaptable compounds that may prove to be of crucial importance to the advancement of phosphor design [9]. Zirconium oxide is distinguished from other materials by its high transparency to both visible and ultraviolet light, as well as its high refractive index, which renders it an excellent candidate for use in a variety of optical applications [10]. ZrO₂ has been shown to serve as an effective host for a diverse array of rare earth ions in luminous applications [11]. Furthermore, given that a high phonon energy of the host material may facilitate non-radiative relaxation, a lower phonon energy of the host material is essential for the development of an efficacious luminous device.

ZrO2, with its low phonon energy (470 cm-1), is a key candidate for enhancing radiative transitions in doped rare-earth ions. Its distinct properties, including a high exciton binding energy and direct band gap (∼5 eV), further strengthen its suitability for optoelectronic applications [12]. Consequently, when lanthanide ions doped into a suitable host material such as ZrO2, the nanophosphors containing lanthanide exhibit remarkable performance for photonic applications. The stabilization of the cubic and/or tetragonal phase of zirconia is facilitated by the addition of several divalent or trivalent cationic stabilizers. Furthermore, stabilization impacts the specific surface area and the temperature at which crystallization or phase transitions occur. The thermomechanical properties of doped zirconia may be enhanced by co-doping with multiple rare earth elements, which may prevent the reduction of grain size. Co-doping balances lattice strain in ZrO2, preventing phase transitions and enhancing thermomechanical stability. This is critical for sensor reproducibility in biological environments [13]. Besides, co-doping with several rare-earth ions enhances the energy transfer mechanism, leading to improved overall luminescence efficiency, stability, and sensitivity [14]. Lanthanide ions are incorporated into the ZrO2 lattice to create luminescence centers. The emission of light by these ions is contingent upon the absorption of energy and the occurrence of electron exchanges [15].

Microwave-assisted synthesis is preferred for nanoparticles production due to its efficiency, rapid reaction times, and ability to synthesize diverse materials. Unlike conventional methods, it utilizes microwave radiation to induce localized heating via ionic conduction and dipole interactions, accelerating reactions through direct energy transfer. This approach enhances reaction rates and precision in material synthesis [16,17].

A significant quantity of nanofiber samples has been produced recently via the electrospinning method. The appeal of nanofibers lies in their capacity to exhibit notable alterations in physical properties when their diameter is reduced to the nanoscale [18]. Immobilizing nanoparticles onto solid substrates (e.g., polymer films) via covalent bonding, electrostatic interactions, or layer-by-layer assembly enables durable sensors with reliable performance for precise detection and monitoring applications. This robust integration enhances measurement stability while maintaining analytical precision [19]. A number of characteristics are necessary to ensure stable conditions, including mechanical stability, thermal stability, chemical inertness, cost-effectiveness, and high porosity. The flexibility, transparency, and ease of processing of polymer films, exemplified by materials such as polyacrylonitrile, polyethylene terephthalate, and polydimethylsiloxane, render them suitable for use as substrates in a diverse range of sensor applications, including wearable sensors and flexible electronic devices [20]. Electrospun nanofibers surpass substrates like paper sensors due to their fine fiber diameter, high porosity, and tunable properties, enabling enhanced sensitivity, mechanical integrity, and stability. These attributes make them ideal for diverse applications requiring precise control over material performance [21]. The enhanced performance can be attributed to two primary factors: the increased surface area and the relatively straightforward delivery of analytes to the active sites on each nanofiber.

In the present study, ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles were synthesized via a microwave-assisted approach, resulting in a luminescent material with a bright orange fluorescent color under 254 nm UV light. The microwave-assisted synthesis method was employed due to its reduced energy consumption, narrow size distribution, and shortened reaction time. The synthesized ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles were employed to ascertain the concentration of levodopa. Changes in emitted color were quantified and qualitatively analyzed using a fluorescence spectrophotometer and a color detector application, respectively, which analyze RGB data. The PAN/ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ sensor was created by electrospinning PAN nanofibers as a substrate for ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+, which was employed to determine levodopa visually.

The chemicals utilized in this study were purchased with high purity and were not subjected to further purification. Zirconium (IV) chloride (ZrCl4, 99.9 %), potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, 99.9 %), dipotassium hydrogen phosphate (K2HPO4, 99.9 %), terbium nitrate pentahydrate (Tb(NO3)3·5H2O, 99.9 %), europium nitrate pentahydrate (Eu(NO3)3·5H2O, 99.9 %), glucose (C6H12O6, 99.5 %), L-glutamine (L-Glu, 97 %), L-cysteine (L-Cys, 99 %), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, 99.7 %), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, 99.9 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.9 %), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.9 %), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4, 99.9 %), potassium chloride (KCl, 99.9 %), manganese(II) chloride (MnCl2, 99.9 %) and zinc acetate (ZnC4H6O4, 99 %) were acquired from Merck. Moreover, calcium chloride (CaCl2, 99 %), ethanol (C2H5OH, 96 %), ascorbic acid (AA, 99 %) were procured from Neutron, Qatran Chemi, and Merck, respectively. Dopamine (98 %) and levodopa (98 %) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich.

A Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (BRUKER Tensor 27) was employed to characterize the functional groups and chemical bonds of the synthesized nanoparticles. The X-ray diffraction pattern (XRD) of the compounds was examined using the PHILIPS-PW1730. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied using the SPECS UHV analysis equipment to assess the surface chemistry. The diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS S-4100 SCINCO) was provided to assess the optical characteristics and bandgap energy of the products. To gain insight into the structural characteristics of these nanoparticles, as well as their size and shape, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), MIRA 3-XMU, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), the FEI TECNAI F20 were employed. Furthermore, the TESCAN MIRA II was equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) on the FESEM instrument, allowing for the verification of the purity of the final product. The nanoparticles were produced by a microwave-assisted approach, utilizing a MILESTONE MICROSYNTH and ATRA furnace for the calcination process. The optical characteristics of the nanoparticles were examined using the Jasco V-750 UV-visible spectrophotometer and the Jasco FP 8300 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Microwave-assisted synthesis was employed to synthesize ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles. Initially, a solution of 0.01 M ZrCl4 was combined with a solution of 0.0005 M each of Tb(NO3)3·6H2O and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O in 50 mL of deionized water. The molar ratio of Eu3+ to Zr4+ in this reaction was 5, which is identical to the molar ratio of Tb3+ to Zr4+. The total molar ratio of Eu3+ and Tb3+ to Zr4+ was calculated to be 10. The final solution was agitated for a period of 30 min.

Subsequently, a white precipitate was produced by the gradual addition of 0.5 M NaOH to the solution until the pH reached 11. A total of 2 mL of NaOH was utilized in the aforementioned reaction. The mixture was then stirred for a further half hour. The final product was subjected to ten cycles of microwave radiation at a power of 650 W, with a duration of one minute on and twenty seconds off. The precipitates were separated by centrifugation and subsequently washed three times with deionized water and three times with ethanol at 7000 rpm for five minutes each time. Finally, the white precipitates were dried at 60 °C for a period of ten hours and subjected to calcination at 900 °C for a period of two hours. The synthesis process is depicted in Scheme 1 .

The objective of this section was to examine the effective factors of the ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles solution on sensing mechanisms, including incubation time, pH, and concentration, with the goal of identifying the optimal conditions for interaction with levodopa. To ascertain the influence of the variables on levodopa measurement and to minimize the error, the central point has been replicated three times. Table S1 presents the matrix proposed by the design expert software. According to the Table S1, optimal condition for determining levodopa were obtained in 365 ppm concentration of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles, pH=7, and 3 min incubation time.

The quantum yield of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles was examined using rhodamine B (10–6 M, QY=31 %) as a reference compound and 50 ppm solution of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles. Absorption spectra of both solutions were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer in order to calculate As and Ar, representing absorbance of the reference and nanoparticles solutions, respectively. Additionally, the fluorescence spectra of these solutions were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 234 nm. Moreover, the integrated fluorescence intensity of both solutions was determined, providing a measure of the area under the spectrum [22].

The fluorescence spectra of the ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+nanoparticles solution were recorded at ambient temperature. A solution of levodopa at varying concentrations (1 nM to 2000 μM) was added to a solution of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles at a ratio of 1.5 mL:1.5 mL. The final solution, comprising a maximum volume of 3 mL, was incubated for a period of 3 min. Both samples were prepared using a pH=7 phosphate buffer, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded with an excitation wavelength of 234 nm.

Samples including L-glutamine, L-cysteine, ascorbic acid, citric acid, sodium chloride, calcium chloride, urea, and glucose were employed to assess the selectivity of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+. A solution of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ at pH=7 was prepared in phosphate buffer, containing 1.5 mL of the solution. Subsequently, 1.5 mL of the aforementioned samples at a concentration of 500 μM was added and incubated for a period of 3 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra at an excitation wavelength of 234 nm and an emission wavelength of 542 nm were recorded.

The sensor was also subjected to examination in human blood serum and urine samples. The samples were then filtered through a syringe filter and centrifuged at 7500 RPM in order to purify them. Subsequently, blood serum and urine samples were diluted 50-fold with phosphate buffer. Subsequently, 1.5 mL of levodopa (5, 10, 25, 50, and 100 μM) in human blood serum and urine samples was prepared and added to 1.5 mL of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ solution at pH=7 and incubated for 3 min. Finally, the fluorescence spectra were recorded using an excitation wavelength of 234 nm.

An appropriate technique for creating woven and non-woven fibers with a diameter of less than 1 μM is electrostatic fiber creation, also referred to as electrospinning. Two distinct processes were involved in the preparation of PAN/ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ composite nanofibers. The electrospinning of PAN nanofibers was initially performed in accordance with the methodology outlined in Table S2 [23]. Subsequently, 30 mg of nanoparticles were immersed in 5 mL ethanol in an ultrasonic bath for 10 min, after which they were sprayed over the nanofibers to stabilize the nanoparticles. The nanofibers were then dried in an oven. The fabrication process of nanofibers is summarized in Scheme 2 .

The functional groups and purity of ZrO2 and ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+nanoparticles were evaluated using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra, as shown in Fig. 1 . The bands observed at 3431 cm-1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of the hydroxide group. Moreover, the hydroxide bending vibrations were observed, as evidenced by the band between 1614 and 1632 cm-1. Stretching and bending are caused by the vibration-induced angle of the bonds and the distance between the bonds of two atoms, respectively. The Zr-O stretching vibrations are identified at 577 and 750 cm-1 [24]. A noticeable change in the intensity of the Zr-O stretching vibration at 750 cm-1 has been occurred, which is due to the incorporation of lanthanide ions [25]. The zirconia tetragonal phase is distinguished by the presence of peaks at 700, 501, 470–476, 450, and 400 cm-1, while monoclinic phases of Zr-O are observed at 618 cm-1 [26]. Furthermore, the ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles spectrum displays bands between 431 and 680 cm-1, which are corroborated by XRD data and indicate the tetragonal phase and the Zr–O vibration bond state in ZrO2 [27–29].

Fig. 2 depicts the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles, generated via the microwave approach. The crystal structure of the compound was determined by examining the pattern. The tetragonal and monoclinic phases were verified using JCPDS card numbers 79–1770 and 37–1484, respectively. Moreover, research has demonstrated that when ZrO2 is co-doped with lanthanides, tetragonal zirconia with crystallite sizes between 6 and 9 nm was stabilized at high temperatures [13]. The conventional lattice constants for the monoclinic phase were determined to be a = 5.1430°, b = 10.2040°, and c = 8.1440°, while for the tetragonal phase they were a = 3.5936°, b = 3.5936°, and c = 5.1814°, as determined by using the Xpert Highscore Plus software. The (031) and (130) planes, represented by the peaks observed at 2θ=28.4° and 2θ=31.5°, respectively, correspond to the monoclinic phase, with lattice spacings of 3.13 and 2.83 nm, respectively. Moreover, the (101), (002), (110), (112), (200), (103), and (211) planes correspond to the tetragonal phase at 2θ=30°, 2θ=34°, 2θ=35°, 2θ=50.2°, 2θ=50.7°, 2θ=59°, and 2θ=60°, respectively. The aforementioned planes are separated by lattice spacings of2.95, 2.59, 2.54, 1.81, 1.79, 1.55, and 1.53 nm, respectively. It is likely that the stabilization of the tetragonal phase is dependent on the ionic radius of each relevant ion. Also, it was observed that the crystal size is not influenced by the type and concentration of lanthanides [30]. In the case of isovalent substitution of Zr4+ in the host, the discrepancy between the ionic radii of the dopant and host cations results in distortion of the crystal lattice, ultimately leading to a phase change. The process of charge compensation occurs when subvalent doping is carried out by introducing oxygen vacancies into the system. It is anticipated that this will have a significant stabilizing effect on the tetragonal and cubic ZrO2 phases [31]. In summary, the introduction of a trivalent co-dopant enhances structural stability and minimizes the long-term aging of co-doped stabilized zirconia. Rare earth ions like terbium and europium also contribute to the stabilization of the zirconia phase [32].

Moreover, the Debye-Scherr equation (D = Kλ/βcosθ) was employed to ascertain the mean crystal size of the nanoparticles. The average crystal size is denoted by D, the Debye-Scherr constant, K, which is equal to 0.9, the X-ray radiation wavelength, λ, the diffraction angle, θ, and the peak width at half height (FWHM), β. The calculations indicate that ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles have an average crystal size of 43 nm [33].

The spherical shape, uniform size distribution, and lack of aggregation of the particles were confirmed by the FESEM images presented in Fig. 3 . To ascertain the purity of the synthesized nanoparticles, the EDS and elemental mapping of these nanoparticles were conducted and are presented in Fig. S1.

The shape and homogeneous size distribution of the nanoparticles were further corroborated by the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images, as illustrated in Fig. 4(a) and 4(b). Additionally, the crystal lattice distances of the nanoparticles were determined, as presented in Fig. 4(c) and 4(d). These values correlate with those observed in the XRD pattern. In image processing, the fast Fourier transform (FFT) is a useful technique for determining the crystal structure [34]. The FFT patterns of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 4(e) and 4(f). The bright spots in this pattern indicate the repeated planes and confirm the crystal structure, which is comparable to the XRD pattern. The [110] planes originate from the lattice spacing of 0.265 nm, as it is shown in Fig. 4
(c), indicating a tetragonal phase with (002), (112), and (110) orientations, as observed in Fig. 4(e). In addition, lattice spacings of 0.25, 0.32, and 0.23 nm have been obtained in Fig. 4(d), indicating [110] planes of monoclinic phase in the (220), (111), and (002) directions, as shown in Fig. 4 (f) [35,36].

The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 5(a)) illustrates the presence of both tetragonal and monoclinic ZrO2 crystal structures. The Miller indices corresponding to the tetragonal ZrO2 or 79–1770 JCPDS card are displayed in the sporadic rings around the central point in Fig. 5
(a). The appearance of the tetrahedral phase is indicated in this pattern by the presence of the distinctive Debye-Scherrer rings that are characteristic of the tetrahedral structure. In addition to the aforementioned circles, a collection of points is also discernible. In accordance with the 1484–37 JCPDS card, these spots were found to be linked to the monoclinic phase of ZrO2. It is important to note that the diffraction rings are discontinuous and contain spots with a very small crystallite size. As shown in Fig. 5(b), the size distribution of spherical ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles with a standard deviation of 1.54 was obtained from a total of 35 measurements in the 6–14 nm range. As can be seen from the HRTEM images, the microwave approach was used to synthesize these nanoparticles without causing agglomeration. The particle size distribution diagram also indicates that spherical nanoparticles with a size of 9.91 nanometers are the most prevalent type of nanoparticle.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) is the most popular technique for assessing surface chemistry, bond structure, surface composition, and interfaces [37]. Fig. 6
(a) depicts the comprehensive XPS survey spectrum of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles, which illustrates the characteristic peaks of the constituent elements, including zirconium, oxygen, europium, and terbium. The binding energies of 181.9 and 184.3 eV, with a difference of 2.4 eV, correspond to Zr3d5/2 and Zr3d3/2 in ZrO2 [38]. Additionally, the binding energies of 182.9 and 185.3 eV, with a 2.4 eV energy difference, correspond to Zr3d5/2 and Zr3d3/2 in zirconium hydroxide (Zr-OH), which are spin-orbit doublet peaks related to zirconium as illustrated in Fig. 6(b) [39]. In Fig. 6(c), the peak centered at 151 eV is attributed to the Tb 4d and is comprised of two peaks, one belonging to Tb3+ and the other to Tb4+. The binding energies of these peaks are 147.5 and 151.2 eV, respectively [40]. It is crucial to note that the two emissions associated with the terbium 4d3/2 and 4d5/2 states are indistinguishable from one another due to their nearly equal binding energies. The emission of photoelectrons from 4F8 to a considerably more stable half-filled 4F7, with a longer electron lifetime than Tb4+ emission, is linked to the sharp peak that corresponds to Tb3+. The oxidation state transition from Tb3+ to Tb4+ in europium- and terbium-co-doped zirconia is driven by redox processes, where Tb3+ ions undergo oxidation under specific conditions. This transformation is facilitated by multiple factors, including: (1) thermal treatments (e.g., high-temperature annealing in oxidizing atmospheres such as air or oxygen), which enhance ion mobility and electron transfer; (2) ambient air exposure, introducing oxygen as an oxidizing agent; (3) defects in the zirconia matrix, particularly oxygen vacancies inherent to ZrO2’s structure, which alter the local charge balance and promote terbium oxidation; and (4) impurity effects, such as aliovalent dopants (e.g., Eu3+) or charge-compensating species that stabilize Tb4+ through redox interactions. The interplay of these factors not only governs the Tb3+/Tb4+ equilibrium but also influences the material’s redox activity and structural stability, making defect engineering and doping strategies critical for tailoring zirconia’s functional properties [14]. Consequently, XPS analysis demonstrated that Tb was present in the molecule in both the +3 and +4 oxidation states [41]. Fig. 6(d) examines the peak associated with Eu3+, which is comprised of two peaks, Eu 3d5/2 and Eu 3d3/2, as a result of the spin-orbit splitting, and is centered at 1134.8 and 1166 eV [42]. Moreover, it can be observed that Eu3+ is not transformed to Eu2+. This is evidenced by the absence of Eu2+ in the major peak [43]. Fig. 6(e) depicts the O1s spectrum, which is centered at 531 eV. The deconvoluted peaks of the O1s spectrum, which are associated with ZrO2, Tb2O3, and Eu2O3, were observed at binding energies of 530.4, 531.2, and 534.5 eV, respectively. The presence of Zr-OH and absorbed OH peaks at 532.4–532.7 eV suggests that reabsorbed OH, during the brief (<5 min) transfer to pretreat the sample, is responsible for the less intense signal observed at higher binding energy (532.7–533 eV) [39,40,42].

Fig. 7
(a) displays the diffuse reflectance spectrum (DRS) of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ and ZrO2 nanoparticles. This indicates that the optical properties of the material are significantly affected by the addition of lanthanides to the ZrO2, particularly with regard to the transitions from the valence band to the conduction band. The shift from the valence band to the conduction band is the primary factor responsible for the apparent reflection. Moreover, extrinsic state transitions, such as those associated with defects, surface traps, or impurities, are responsible for reflection in the UV-visible spectrum. It is well established that the distribution of defects is more observable on the surface of particles with a higher surface-to-volume ratio and smaller size. Consequently, the reflection is less pronounced in particles with smaller dimensions [44]. The spectrum, which was recorded between 200 and 800 nm, illustrates the reflection of samples at a specific wavelength. The figure illustrates an energy absorption band of ZrO2, ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ in 240 nm, which exhibits a variation in intensity with the quantity of lanthanide present. The addition of lanthanide to ZrO2 results in a reduction in band gap energy and an increase in redshift. The ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles displays absorption within the 390–590 nm range, which is ascribed to f–d electron transitions [45]. A comparison of ZrO2 to ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ revealed a redshift in the absorption edge of ZrO2, a phenomenon attributed to an increase in the wavelength of the spectra [46].

Fig. 7(b) depicts the excitation and emission spectra of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles, represented in blue and orange, respectively. To comprehend the inherent photoluminescence characteristics of the host ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles, the photoluminescence emission and excitation spectra acquired at room temperature were consulted. The ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ exhibits an excitation band of 200–300 nm, centered at 234 nm, as shown by the PL excitation spectra with monitoring. 542 nm emission wavelength was used to examine the PL emission spectrum of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles. The spectra illustrate the fluorescence emission corresponding to the europium and terbium ion transitions. ZrO2 as host band is responsible for the peak observed between 300 and 500 nm, which may be associated with the Zr4+– O2 − charge transfer state. In this state, electrons are excited from the 2p orbitals of the surrounding O2 − ions to the unoccupied 4d orbitals of Zr4+ within the zirconium–oxygen octahedral unit. The interaction of Eu3+/Tb3+ions with ZrO2 involves the formation of oxygen vacancies, which may trap these ions and modulate their luminescence efficiency. These vacancies enhance energy transfer from the ZrO2 lattice to Eu3+/Tb3+ ions, intensifying red/green emissions. Additionally, the excitation peak at 234 nm corresponds to the charge transfer state (CTS) between O2- (2p orbitals of zirconia) and Eu3+ (4f orbitals), as well as the 4f–5d transition of Tb3+, further confirming their synergistic interaction. In accordance with the Pauling electronegativity scale, europium exhibits a greater electronegativity (1.2) than terbium (1.1). Consequently, the incorporation of these dopants into the zirconia lattice results in an enhanced covalence, which induces a relative shift in the excitation band towards undoped zirconia. The spectra of the 5 % mol Tb3+/ 5 % mol Eu3+ doped samples display three distinct bands. The host material is distinguished by the presence of a broad band in the initial band. The second is a set of Tb3+-related bands, which occur at 542 nm. Finally, there are the intra-configurational transition lines of the Eu3+ ion, which appear at 591 and 614 nm [14]. The greatest emission intensity of the terbium electric dipole transition, 5D4→7F5, is observed at 542 nm, and the optimal wavelength for excitation is 234 nm. The parity-allowed magnetic dipole-induced 5D0→7F1 transition occurs at a wavelength of 591 nm, while the electric dipole-permitted 5D0→7F2 transition, the primary emission line originating from europium, occurs at 614 nm [47]. As the concentration of Tb3+ ions increase, both the donor (Tb3+ ion) and the activator (Eu3+ ion) exhibit a reduction in emission intensity. This phenomenon could potentially be explained by structural distortion or a combination of tetragonal and monoclinic phases. The phenomenon of energy transfer between Tb3+ and Eu3+ is most commonly explained by the hypothesis that when the concentration of the acceptor (Eu3+) ion is subject to change, the emission intensity of the acceptor increases while that of the donor decreases. It is also noteworthy that at 542 nm, the overall emission intensity of Tb3+ demonstrated an increase when the concentrations of the donor and acceptor ions were in equilibrium. This evidence indicates that an elevated donor concentration increases the probability of donor ion emission, rather than further enhancing energy transfer from Tb3+ to Eu3+ [14].

Fig. S2(a) depicts the time-dependent stability of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles. A 30-day timeframe was employed to verify the stability of the nanoparticles over time. As can be observed, the fluorescence intensity of these nanoparticles remains constant over time. Consequently, the stability of nanoparticles enables their use as sensors over an extended period. Fig. S2(b) illustrates the optical stability of these nanoparticles. The dispersed nanoparticles were settled in a buffer at an optimal concentration under UV light with a wavelength of 365 nm for 60 min in order to examine the optical stability. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of the nanoparticles exhibited a slight change when subjected to UV light. Furthermore, the stability of nanoparticles in the presence of interfering substances, such as NaCl, was examined and is presented in Fig. S2(c). To verify the stability, solutions containing 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5, and 1 µM NaCl were employed [48]. Consequently, an 8 % reduction in stability was observed after 30 days, with a further 9 % reduction in the presence of NaCl and a 22 % decline following exposure to UV light. The results demonstrated that these nanoparticles exhibited good stability when the interfering substance was present. It can be concluded that these nanoparticles exhibit good stability under a variety of circumstances.

The quantum yield equation (Qs = Qr × (Ar/As) × (Is/Ir) × (ns/nr)2) was employed to assess the photon emission and absorption intensities of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles in comparison to a reference sample, thereby determining their quantum yield. Rhodamine B was used as a reference sample with a quantum efficiency of 31 % [22]. Ar and As indicate the absorbance of the reference and the nanoparticles, respectively. The refractive indexes of the solvent for the sample and reference solutions, respectively, are represented by ηs and ηr. The solvent is double distilled water with a refractive index of 1.33. Ir is the integral under the fluorescence spectrum of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+, and Is is the integral under the fluorescence spectrum of the reference sample at the 234 and 500 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively [48]. To reduce the impacts of self-absorption, diluted samples with an absorption of less than 0.1 were employed. Table S3 shows the calculated data. ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ have a quantum efficiency of 60 %.

An examination of the fluorescence quenching process of the ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles revealed that quenching occurs subsequent to the sensor's interaction with levodopa. This is due to the fact that levodopa undergoes oxidation and subsequent conversion to dopaquinone when exposed to an alkaline environment. In this interaction, the sensor acts as an electron donor, while dopaquinone acts as an electron acceptor. It is important to note that levodopa is not converted to dopaquinone in acidic environments. Following oxidation in an alkaline environment, levodopa absorbs the excited electrons of the sensor, preventing them from returning to the ground state. The photoinduced electron transfer (PET) mechanism, a form of dynamic quenching, provides a theoretical basis for this action, which results in fluorescence quenching [48,49]. The quenching of a fluorophore's fluorescence by PET is a consequence of the light-induced transfer of an electron from an electron-rich species to an electron-deficient species [50]. The fluorescence of the fluorophores can typically be quenched by PET, which involves a shift in electron density from an electron donor to an electron acceptor [51]. In Eu3+/Tb3+ co-doped zirconia systems, energy transfer predominantly occurs via a weak quadrupole-quadrupole interaction, where Tb3+ (donor) transfers its excited-state energy to Eu3+ (acceptor). When levodopa interacts with the co-doped zirconia, it alters the energy transfer dynamics by influencing the efficiency of Tb3+-to- Eu3+ energy migration through non-radiative pathways. In the PET mechanism, excitation energy is non-radiatively transferred from Tb3+ to Eu3+. The addition of levodopa perturbs this process by interacting with the excited states of the donor (Tb3+) or acceptor (Eu3+) ions, creating non-radiative decay channels that quench the luminescence intensity [52,53]. The Stern-Volmer equation is widely used to validate dynamic quenching mechanisms, which can be experimentally confirmed through various methods. A key approach involves investigating the temperature dependence of the interaction between levodopa and the ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles. According to the Stern-Volmer formalism for dynamic quenching, the quenching rate constant exhibits a positive correlation with temperature, as elevated thermal energy enhances collision frequency between the fluorophore and quencher. This temperature-dependent behavior provides direct evidence for dynamic quenching in the system [54]. Fig. 8
(a) illustrates the interaction between ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles and levodopa at temperatures of 30 °C, 40 °C, and 50 °C. The quenching rate exhibits a linear increase with rising temperature, thereby confirming the PET mechanism as the dominant pathway. To further validate this mechanism, UV–Vis spectroscopy was employed to analyze the absorption spectra of the nanoparticles before and after levodopa addition (Fig. 8(b)). A distinct shift in absorption intensity at a fixed wavelength is observed upon levodopa introduction, which aligns with the characteristic spectral changes associated with PET-driven quenching. This behavior provides additional evidence for electron transfer between the nanoparticles and levodopa, corroborating the proposed mechanism [55].

In examining the impact of levodopa on the optical characteristics of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles, it is essential to consider the variables influencing the measurement of levodopa. Design Expert 13 software was employed to facilitate optimization, which can be utilized to examine the influence of these parameters on the design circumstances. A three-factor study was conducted to investigate the fluorescence quenching of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles using an optimal design method. In this design, the incubation time, pH, and nanoparticle concentration were investigated as useful variables to obtain the optimal response with the greatest amount of levodopa-sensor interaction. Fig. S3(a) depicts the two crucial variables, pH and nanoparticle concentration, in the interaction between levodopa and nanoparticles. The sensor exhibits enhanced responsiveness to levodopa when the pH is elevated within the range of 7–10, and the concentration of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles is augmented to a range of 250–450 ppm. Fig. S3(b) illustrates the combined impact of pH and time on the contour and surface curve, demonstrating that within the 3–7 min time frame, a 7 min incubation time increases the interaction between ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ and levodopa. Furthermore, the diagram illustrates that the interaction between these nanoparticles and levodopa is enhanced at pH=10, which is within the range of 7 to 10. Fig. S3(c) depicts the growth in response of the sensor as the concentration of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ and the incubation time with levodopa increase. Both the concentration of nanoparticles and the incubation time were examined within the range of 250–450 ppm and 3–7 min, respectively. The optimal pH and incubation time are critical for maximizing sensor performance. At pH = 10, levodopa (pKa ∼8.7–10.5) exists predominantly in its deprotonated, negatively charged form due to the ionization of its phenolic and carboxylic groups. This enhances electrostatic interactions with the positively charged sensor surface (e.g., via hydroxyl groups on zirconia or doped metal sites), promoting efficient adsorption and electron transfer. Conversely, at lower pH, protonation of levodopa reduces its ionization, weakening surface binding and signal response. The 7-minute incubation period was selected to balance reaction kinetics and stability. Shorter durations (<5 min) lead to incomplete analyte adsorption, while prolonged exposure (>10 min) risks nonspecific binding, oxidation of levodopa in alkaline media, or sensor surface fouling. This timeframe ensures sufficient analyte-surface interaction for precise detection while minimizing background noise and degradation [56]. The chemical environment and contact time are balanced by these optimal parameters, which together provide the best feasible sensor performance.

The examination of p-values and lack of fit is presented in Table S4. The P-value, which is derived through statistical measurement, serves to assess the significance of the model's terms and effects. The probability of detecting an effect due to random chance is indicated by the p-value associated with each design. A lower p-value indicates a greater influence of the component under investigation on the answer. A large lack of fit suggests that the model may include unknown components or interactions which may require further investigation to enhance the precision of the model [57]. Table S5 indicates that the R2 value for levodopa measurements is 0.9922.

It is evident from normal plot of residuals, as shown in Fig. S4(a) that predicted values that are fairly close to the actual ones, contributing to the fact that the work was done with lowest error. Furthermore, distribution of results in the presence of a particular error is displayed in the residual vs predicted chart, as shown in Fig. S4(b), indicating better distribution of values by the proximity to the average line and the absence of outliers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of sensitivity and selectivity of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ sensor
ce:section.ce:para:
Levodopa solutions with concentrations between 1 nM and 2000 μM were prepared and added to the sensor at an optimal concentration in order to determine the sensor's sensitivity. Consequently, a buffer solution with a pH of 7 was employed to generate a solution of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles at appropriate concentrations. Following the addition of levodopa at varying doses, the fluorescence spectrum was recorded using an excitation wavelength of 234 nm. The acquired results, depicted in Fig. 9
(a), indicate that the fluorescence intensity of the sensor decreases more with an increase in levodopa concentration and that the quenching intensity of fluorescence decreases in proportion to the decrease in levodopa concentration. A linear equation ((F0/F)-1 = 2.1172Cl-dopa+0.2425) with a correlation coefficient of 0.982 was generated using the Stern-Volmer equation within the concentration range of 1–100 μM levodopa. The equation LOD = 3δ/Ksv was employed to compute the limit of detection (LOD) of the ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ sensor, resulting in a value of 91 nM. In this formula, Ksv represents the slope of the calibration curve, as illustrated in Fig. 9(b). δ, on the other hand, is the standard deviation of the sensor in the absence of an analyte, as determined through five repetitions [58]. The sensitivity of this study has been enhanced in comparison to the previous research conducted by our team. For instance, sulfur- and nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots were employed for the determination of levodopa using a fluorescence sensor, resulting in an LOD of 0.45 μM [48]. Similarly, the fluorescence sensing of levodopa using LiSrVO4:Eu3+ has been demonstrated to achieve an LOD of 279 nM [59]. As evidenced in this study, ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles have been shown to demonstrate a lower LOD and higher sensitivity in the detection of levodopa based on fluorescence sensing. Table S6 summarizes the analytical performance of our ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ fluorescence sensor alongside existing methods for levodopa detection. Our sensor achieves a lower detection limit (LOD = 91 nM) compared to most reported techniques while maintaining a wide linear range (1–100 μM) suitable for clinical applications. Notably, the sensor eliminates the need for complex instrumentation or time-consuming sample pretreatment, offering a portable and cost-effective solution for on-site analysis. Furthermore, the dual-mode detection capability (quantitative fluorescence quenching and qualitative colorimetric readout) distinguishes it from single-signal approaches, enabling rapid visual assessment in resource-limited settings.

In order to assess the selectivity of the sensors in comparison to levodopa, a number of other interferents were employed, including L-glutamine, L-cysteine, ascorbic acid, citric acid, sodium chloride, calcium chloride, urea, and glucose [59]. As shown in Fig. 10 , the sensor exhibited a pronounced decrease in fluorescence intensity in response to levodopa, whereas the presence of interfering agents in place of levodopa had no discernible impact on the sensor's fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 10, the fluorescence intensity of ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3++LD remained stable even at interferent concentrations higher than physiological levels. This indicates that the sensor’s recognition of LD is minimally affected by ionic strength variations, likely due to strong LD-surface binding. The catechol and carboxyl groups in LD form specific coordination bonds with the zirconia matrix, outweighing nonspecific electrostatic interactions with Na+/Ca2+. Also, the Eu3+/Tb3+dopants are embedded within the ZrO2 lattice, reducing susceptibility to surface-bound ions. Consequently, it is claimed that the sensor is selective for levodopa. Fig. 10 illustrates that levodopa exerts a considerably more pronounced influence on sensor quenching than interventionists [60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of levodopa in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of various concentrations of levodopa in human blood serum and urine samples, in conjunction with the standard, was investigated to demonstrate that the fluorescence sensors are unaffected by the presence of interfering compounds that are frequently encountered in biological samples [61]. Table 1 presents the computed concentration of levodopa in real samples, accompanied by the levodopa recovery percentage and the relative standard deviations. To ascertain the calculated concentration, a calibration curve was employed. The concentrations of levodopa employed in this study were 5, 10, 25, 50, and 100 μM. The fluorescence sensor is capable of accurately detecting levodopa in real samples, as evidenced by the high recovery percentages of 99–102 % and 86–99 % in blood serum and urine samples, respectively. The standard deviation of the measurements of levodopa in blood serum and urine samples in five replicates was found to be between 0.9 and 2.3 % and between 2.9 and 4.5 %, respectively. This suggests that the sensor's error in measuring levodopa in the samples is actual. These findings demonstrate that the sensor is capable of measuring levodopa with good consistency in a complex environment [62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrospun substrate as fluorimeter for rapid levodopa detection
ce:section.ce:para:
The stabilization of nanoparticles on paper substrates is not a viable option due to the difficulty of preparing a homogeneous solution. The findings of the studies indicate that polymer-stabilized nanoparticles result in the production of superior sensors [63]. In order to deposit nanoparticles on the fibers as outlined in Table S2, the electrospinning process was employed, resulting in the formation of nanofibers. Subsequently, ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles were applied to PAN nanofibers via a spraying technique. FESEM and elemental mapping were utilized to analyze the nanofibers and the elements they contained, with the objective of investigating the structure of the nanofibers as a substrate for the nanoparticles. As presented in Fig. 11
(a) and 11(b), the fabrication of uniformly thin, beadless, electrospun PAN fibers can be regarded as a process for producing nanoparticle substrates. The diameter of these fibers was measured to be 554 nm. Fig. 11(c) and 11(d) shows the nanofibers following the spraying of nanoparticles. As can be observed, the uniform distribution of nanoparticles on the surface of nanofibers is confirmed. Moreover, the elemental mapping of electrospun nanofibers as a substrate for ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles is depicted in Fig. S5.

Fig. 12
(a) displays images of the sensors, which are composed of PAN nanofibers serving as a substrate for ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles. PAN nanofibers were subjected to a spray coating process utilizing a nanoparticle solution in a volatile solvent, such as ethanol. Following a visual inspection of the fluorescence properties of the substrates using a UV lamp with a wavelength of 254 nm, levodopa solution in varying concentrations (1–2000 µM) was employed to observe the variation in the quenching intensity of the sensor. Levodopa was subsequently added to the PAN electrospun nanofibers at various concentrations in order to ascertain the color intensity. As illustrated in Fig. 12(a), the images captured using an iPhone 11 smartphone demonstrate a decrease in ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ fluorescence intensity as the concentration of levodopa rises. Although the color variations are discernible, a color recognizer application (Color Picker) was employed to utilize the orange color of the case in order to evaluate the RGB values, thus increasing the accuracy of the procedure and preventing deviations due to visual clarity. The data obtained from the Color Picker are displayed on the calibration curve (Fig. 12(b)), based on various levodopa values. The correlation coefficient for the ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ sensor was obtained at 0.98 in the linear range of 0–100 μM according to the Stern-Volmer equation, as illustrated in Fig. 12(b).

To elucidate the mechanism of color emission, as shown in Fig. 13 , it is essential to acknowledge that when ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ is subjected to UV light, the ZrO2 matrix is capable of absorbing the energy of the excitation light, resulting in the movement of electrons from the valence band to the conduction band. Subsequently, some of the electrons may traverse the defect level via a non-radiative transition process, thereby returning them from the defect level to the ground state. To enhance the luminescence intensities of Eu3+ and Tb3+, energy is transmitted to them from the host, then electrons from the ground state (4f8) of Tb3+ and (4f6) of Eu3+ are then stimulated to their respective excited states by a non-radiative relaxation [64]. Subsequently, the electrons facilitate multi-phonon processes, enabling relaxation to the lowest excited states, 5D4 for Tb3+ and 5D0 for Eu3+. In the case of Tb3+, electrons in the 5D₄ state may either transfer their excited energy through non-radiative cross-relaxation to an excited energy level of Eu3+ or relax radiatively to the ground states, resulting in the emission of Tb3+ 5D3,4→7F6,5,4,3. Subsequently, a radiative transition from the Eu3+ ion (5F0,1→7D1,2) to the 7FJ levels, which is characteristic of the orange-red spectrum, occurs. The 7F0,1–5D1,2 absorption spectrum of Eu3+ and the 5D3,4→7F6,5,4,3 emission spectrum of Tb3+ overlap, thereby facilitating the cross-relaxation process between the two [65]. As ZrO2 serves as the host for the energy transfer phenomenon, terbium ions operate as the sensitizer and europium ions as the activator [66]. The emission of Tb3+ has been observed at 490 and 542 nm, occurring as a result of the non-radiative decay of electrons to the 5D4 level. Subsequently, the excited ions decay to the 7FJ ground state through transitions from 5D4 to 7F3,4,5,6. The sample predominantly emits green light, whereas the emission of Eu3+ is observed at 591, and 614 nm when its electrons are transported from 5D0 to 7F1 and 7F2, respectively, resulting in the emission of red light [64,67]. The co-doping of Tb3+ and Eu3+ ions in the matrix enables the europium ions to absorb the energy through non-radiative transfer of the excited state of terbium ions. This is due to the fact that the energy levels of the 5D1 and 5D0 states of europium ions are 19,028 cm−1 and 17,277 cm−1, respectively, while those of the 5D3 and 5D4 states of terbium ions are 26,336 cm−1 and 20,545 cm−1, respectively.

As illustrated in Fig. 13, nearly identical differences exist between the terbium ion's relaxation transition level and the europium ion's absorption transition level, as demonstrated below:
5D4(Tb) + 7F1(Eu) + 604 cm−1 → 7F6(Tb) + 5D2(Eu)
5D4(Tb) + 7F0(Eu) + 239 cm−1 → 7F5(Tb) + 5D1(Eu)

The absorbed energy is thus transferred from excited terbium ions to europium ions via the two effective cross-relaxation pathways previously mentioned. As the concentration of europium increases, the distance between the europium and terbium ions decreases, thereby enhancing the probability of excited state terbium energy transfer to europium and keeps Tb3+ from quenching [67]. Moreover, Tb3+ exhibits a slightly reduced ionic radius (92.3 pm) in comparison to Eu3+ (95 pm), coupled with a higher charge density, which ultimately leads to enhanced stability of the Tb3+-levodopa complex in comparison to the Eu3+-levodopa complex. This result is due to the enhanced electrostatic interactions between Tb3+ and the negatively charged oxygen atoms present in the levodopa hydroxyl groups. The formation of strong coordination bonds is contingent upon the effective overlap of the donor atoms' orbitals (nitrogen and oxygen of levodopa) due to the partial filling of the f-orbitals. This enhanced bonding facilitates the stability and efficiency of the Tb3+- Eu3+ energy transfer system, which does not result in the quenching of the Tb3+ ions. This process ensures that the Tb3+ ions remain active and capable of transferring energy to the Eu3+ ions [68]. Conversely, the weaker coordination bonds observed in this study can be attributed to the diminished overlap between the f-electrons of Eu3+ and the levodopa donor atoms when compared to Tb3+. The effective transfer of energy to Eu3+ is facilitated by the robust and stable complex formation that occurs between levodopa and Tb3+. Given the comparatively weaker connection between levodopa and Eu3+, it is Eu3+ that predominantly serves as an acceptor of the energy transmitted from the Tb3+–levodopa complex [14,69]. The effective energy transfer from Tb3+ to Eu3+ is facilitated by levodopa, which causes greater orange emission from Eu3+ in lower levodopa concentrations and slightly green emission from Tb3+ in higher levodopa concentrations. This results in quenched fluorescence at maximum levodopa concentration [68]. The characteristic emission PL intensity of Eu3+ increases monotonically, while that of Tb3+ decreases. These features are characteristic of this phenomenon [67]. Consequently, a single Tb3+ stimulation results in the Eu3+ red emission phenomenon, which can be attributed to the Tb3+ sensitization effect. Tb3+ emits green light under 254 nm excitation light, which gradually changes to yellow and then orange in the ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ composite with Eu3+ doping [70]. Quantitative analysis of analyte concentration is feasible using color change and the range within which it occurs. Additionally, color change can be observed visually and with the naked eye, providing a convenient and accessible method for monitoring analyte concentration. The potential for ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ phosphors to produce adjustable visible light makes them a promising candidate for use in multi-color sensors.

In summary, levodopa was quantified using ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles with bright orange fluorescence emission, synthesized via a microwave-assisted approach with 60 % quantum efficiency. To ascertain the sensor's lowest detection limit (LOD), a series of levodopa concentrations were employed, with the results indicating that the sensor's lowest detection limit fell within 91 nM within the linear range of 1–100 μM. The ZrO2:Eu3+:Tb3+ nanoparticles demonstrated excellent stability and selectivity. The sensor was examined under optimal condition achieved from RSM-optimal matrix using design expert 13 software. The use of real human serum and urine samples for drug assessment enabled the validation of the sensor's performance in the presence of biological components. The recovery percentage of the sensor in blood serum and urine was found to be 86–99 % and 97–102 %, respectively. To validate the PET process of dynamic quenching, temperature and UV–vis tests were employed, and the mechanism of interaction between the sensor and levodopa was explored. Moreover, nanoparticles were deposited onto polyacrylonitrile (PAN) nanofibers produced via electrospinning, and a range of levodopa concentrations were employed to validate the sensor's quenching intensity. The Color Picker application was employed to accurately detect changes in RGB values following the addition of varying concentrations of levodopa, which demonstrated a notable linear correlation in the reduction of fluorescence intensity with increasing levodopa concentration.

Narges Karimi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation.
